Title: CTP synthase is essential for early endosperm development by regulating nuclei spacing

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: CTP synthase domain; glutamine amide transfer domain
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the ATP-dependent transfer of the amide nitrogen from glutamine to UTP to generate CTP in the final step of de novo pyrimidine nucleotide biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Rate-limiting final step in de novo pyrimidine nucleotide biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Required for nucleic acid synthesis and rapid nuclear division during early endosperm development; relevant to cereal grain development and yield.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Developing endosperm during early syncytial stage
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes CTP synthesis, early endosperm nuclear division and nuclei spacing; loss of function abolishes normal endosperm development, whereas overexpression increases grain size and 1000-grain weight.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Map-based cloning and CRISPR alleles showed that OsCTPS1 is the causal gene for the endospermless 2 phenotype. 2. The enl2-1 N86I substitution significantly reduced CTP synthase activity, and null alleles caused severe early syncytial endosperm defects with clumped nuclei that failed to migrate to the periphery. 3. OsCTPS1 is highly expressed in developing seeds and dividing tissues, transiently forms macromolecular structures in early endosperm, interacts with tubulins, and its overexpression increases grain size and grain weight.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Ubiquitously expressed; highest among six CTPS genes in developing seeds at 1 DAP; elevated in shoot apical meristem, panicles, root stele and quiescent center, anthers, veins, embryo, and endosperm.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Ubiquitously expressed; enriched in dividing tissues and early developing endosperm/embryo sac.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm and nucleus; filamentous/macromolecular structures in cytosol of early endosperm; co-localizes with microtubules
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Map-based cloning; mutant sequencing; CRISPR/Cas9 knockout; enzyme activity assay with purified protein; co-immunoprecipitation; promoter-GUS expression analysis; GFP localization; immunolocalization of microtubules; overexpression and grain trait analysis; confocal microscopy of developing endosperm
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: maize ubiquitin 1 promoter (constitutive) for overexpression and OsCTPS1-sGFP; native OsCTPS1 promoter for GUS reporter
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early seed developmental stage (3-96 HAP / 1-3 DAP); DON treatment induced macromolecular structures in protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Public-domain expression data for rice CTPS genes; qRT-PCR expression profiling; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: OsCTPS1 overexpression slightly decreased plant height, while most other agronomic traits were similar to WT; loss-of-function causes severe seed defect and reduced early seedling/root growth.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for increasing grain size and yield by overexpression or tuned activation to promote early endosperm nuclear division; deployment should monitor possible plant-height reduction and avoid knockout/strong loss-of-function alleles.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Besides its metabolic enzymatic role, the paper provides evidence that OsCTPS1 also acts as a structural factor associated with microtubules during nuclei spacing in syncytial endosperm. Macromolecular filament formation is transient and stage-specific in early endosperm. The enl2-1 allele is a hypomorphic missense mutant (N86I), whereas CRISPR alleles behave as nulls and are more severe.
